metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete grep proving command for the ticket-flow AC, e.g. `grep -qE 'GitHub issue|maintainer.*ticket|rebar-ticket' docs/your-first-change.md` (or enumerate the three key phrases as separate checks) so all three loop steps are verifiable.
findings.finding: The AC 'outsider ticket flow is actionable without sibling stories' has no concrete proving command — 'actionable' is a qualitative judgment with no grep or structural check, leaving verification subjective.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — third bullet ('The outsider ticket flow is actionable...')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC: `prove: grep -cE 'GitHub issue' docs/your-first-change.md` ≥ 1 AND `grep -cE 'maintainer' docs/your-first-change.md` ≥ 1 AND `grep -cE 'rebar-ticket:' docs/your-first-change.md` ≥ 1 (this last is already covered by the nine-beat grep, so link to it).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add grep proving commands for ACs 4 and 5, e.g. AC4: `grep -q 'maintainer.*[Ss]ubmit' docs/your-first-change.md`; AC5: `grep -q 'CONTRIBUTING.*2c\|review-policy' docs/your-first-change.md` plus an optional exclusion pattern for sub-reason enumeration.
findings.finding: The 'Submit expectations match reality' and 'No duplicated vote table' ACs describe their proof in prose ('doc states...', 'the doc links... and does not enumerate') but supply no executable grep commands, unlike every other AC.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — fourth bullet ('Submit expectations match reality') and fifth bullet ('No duplicated vote table')
findings.suggested_fix: AC4 prove: `grep -qE 'maintainer (performs|handles|submits)' docs/your-first-change.md`. AC5 prove (link present): `grep -qE 'CONTRIBUTING.*#.*2c|review-policy\.md' docs/your-first-change.md`; the negative claim (no sub-reason table) can be noted as 'human-review only' with that rationale stated explicitly so the AC is not falsely implied to be automatable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the 'open a GitHub issue' ticket-initiation step with a channel that actually works on this mirror (e.g. the SUPPORT.md channel, a Gerrit change description, or email), and verify the mechanism is reachable before specifying it in the AC.
findings.finding: The outsider ticket flow mechanism is broken: the tutorial instructs the outsider to 'open a GitHub issue describing the work,' but .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/config.yml has `blank_issues_enabled: false` — GitHub Issues are explicitly disabled on this repository mirror. An outsider following the tutorial's ticket-flow step cannot open a GitHub issue.
findings.location: What section, beat 3 ('Your first change'): 'open a GitHub issue describing the work; the maintainer files the rebar ticket and replies with the id'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the GitHub-issue step with the SUPPORT.md channel (already listed in .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/config.yml as the support/bug contact link): 'file a request via the support channel (SUPPORT.md); the maintainer files the rebar ticket and replies with the id.' This requires no new infrastructure.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove `git commit -s` from the tutorial's day-one commit beat and instead add it as a cross-link step that the DCO story inserts when that enforcement actually lands, keeping the tutorial's commit command consistent with CONTRIBUTING.md §2a.
findings.finding: The tutorial mandates `git commit -s` (DCO sign-off / Signed-off-by trailer) in beat 3, but this requirement is not enforced by any current CI gate, is absent from CONTRIBUTING.md §2a's commit instructions, and has no presence anywhere in the codebase — it belongs to a future 'DCO story' that is explicitly out-of-scope. Teaching a spurious gating requirement contradicts the 'consistent with CONTRIBUTING.md §1–§2 (same commands)' design constraint the plan states for itself.
findings.location: What section, beat 3 ('Your first change'): 'branch from main → commit with `git commit -s` (DCO sign-off; brief, links CONTRIBUTING's DCO section once the DCO story lands — until then links developercertificate.org)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add grep anchors (or equivalent prose checks) in AC1 for the four unverified beats: account setup (e.g. `rebar.solutions`), HTTP credential generation (e.g. `HTTP Credentials` or `/a/`), clone step (e.g. `git clone`), and branch step (e.g. `git checkout -b` or `git switch -c`).
findings.finding: AC1 states it will 'prove' nine-beat sequence coverage via grep, but only five grep tokens are specified (`refs/for/main`, `commit-msg`, `--amend`, `commit -s`, `rebar-ticket:`), leaving four beats — account setup, credential generation, clone, and branch creation — with no mechanical verification. The criterion's stated proof does not substantiate the full nine-beat claim.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1, nine-beat sequence proof
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the AC1 grep list: `grep -E 'rebar\.solutions|HTTP Credentials' docs/your-first-change.md` (account + credential), `grep -q 'git clone' docs/your-first-change.md` (clone), `grep -E 'checkout -b|switch -c' docs/your-first-change.md` (branch).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either gate the `git commit -s` instruction on the DCO story having already landed (make it a conditional cross-link added by that story, not a day-one requirement), or replace it with a forward-looking note ('a DCO sign-off may be required in the future') that clearly signals it is not currently enforced.
findings.finding: `git commit -s` (DCO sign-off / Signed-off-by) is mandated in the tutorial's 'Your first change' beat, but no DCO enforcement exists in the codebase, CONTRIBUTING.md, or any current CI gate — it is YAGNI: teaching a requirement that serves only a hypothetical future gate (the 'DCO story'), not the current done-definition.
findings.location: What section, beat 3 ('Your first change'): 'branch from main → commit with `git commit -s` (DCO sign-off; brief, links CONTRIBUTING's DCO section once the DCO story lands — until then links developercertificate.org)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a covering AC (or extend AC1's grep list) to verify the gotchas table exists (e.g. `grep -q 'Change-Id' docs/your-first-change.md`) and that the See-also link to CONTRIBUTING.md is present (e.g. `grep -q 'CONTRIBUTING' docs/your-first-change.md`).
findings.finding: Two described deliverable elements — the 'See-also → CONTRIBUTING.md as the full reference' section (beat 5) and the gotchas table (beat 4 with its seven named rows) — are described in the What section but have no covering acceptance criterion, making their presence unverifiable at review time.
findings.location: What section — beats 4 and 5; Acceptance Criteria (all six ACs)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether a 'shepherded-patch' section in CONTRIBUTING.md exists or is owned by a sibling story; either add a note that this path is 'coming soon' / link the owning story, or remove the reference until that section ships, so the tutorial has no broken links on day one.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'for tiny fixes the shepherded-patch path (CONTRIBUTING) skips Gerrit entirely', implying CONTRIBUTING.md already contains a 'shepherded-patch' section. A search of the entire repository returns zero hits for 'shepherded' or 'shepherded-patch'; no such section exists in CONTRIBUTING.md. The tutorial text directs outsiders to this non-existent path, which will result in a dead reference on a live outsider-facing document.
findings.location: What section, beat 3 ('Your first change'): 'for tiny fixes the shepherded-patch path (CONTRIBUTING) skips Gerrit entirely'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'for tiny fixes the shepherded-patch path (CONTRIBUTING) skips Gerrit entirely' with either a forward-reference ('a simplified shepherded-patch path for tiny fixes will be documented in CONTRIBUTING.md — for now, all contributions go through Gerrit') or remove the mention entirely until the owning story ships.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the grep boundary: replace `push gerrit ` with `push gerrit\b` (or `push[[:space:]]gerrit([[:space:]]|$|[^a-z])`) so end-of-line occurrences are caught.
findings.finding: The grep exclusion pattern `push gerrit ` (trailing space) in AC 2 will produce a false-negative: the phrase `push gerrit.` or `push gerrit\n` (end-of-line) would slip through undetected — verify-command defect taxonomy #1 (substring false-negative due to missing boundary anchor).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — second bullet, grep command: `grep -cE "feature-branch-drivers|CLAUDE|dual-remote|push gerrit " docs/your-first-change.md`

findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align the outer story title with the plan body's actual scope — either rename it to match the H1 ('your-first-change tutorial + funnel links') or add a parenthetical '(this story: tutorial + funnel links only)' to prevent backlog-level confusion about what is delivered here vs. in sibling stories.
findings.finding: The outer story title ('your-first-change tutorial, CONTRIBUTING restructure, README front door') lists two items — 'CONTRIBUTING restructure' and 'README front door' — that the plan body explicitly puts OUT of scope ('OUT: … restructuring CONTRIBUTING.md; README.md front-door work (own story: README front door)'). An implementer reading only the title would expect these three deliverables; the body drops two of them without flagging the discrepancy.
findings.location: Story title (line 1) vs. Scope section (OUT bullets)
findings.suggested_fix: Rename the story title to: 'Outsider onboarding: your-first-change tutorial and funnel links' and add a note that CONTRIBUTING restructure and README front door are separate stories in this epic.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/config.yml` to the funnel-link scope (or explicitly exclude it with rationale), since its 'Contribute a change (via Gerrit)' contact link is a parallel outsider first-contact surface to the PR template that the plan already updates.
findings.finding: .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/config.yml is an unenumerated consumer: it contains a 'Contribute a change (via Gerrit)' contact link pointing directly to CONTRIBUTING.md and serves as a GitHub-native outsider first-contact surface parallel to the PR template — yet it is not listed in scope or excluded.
findings.location: Scope section / What section (funnel links); .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/config.yml line 9–11
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, either add `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/config.yml` to IN (update its CONTRIBUTING.md URL or about text to also reference `your-first-change.md`) or add it to OUT with a brief rationale (e.g., 'contact links are config-format, not Markdown — deferred'). Update AC funnel-link checks accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the current-state defaults explicitly, e.g. 'At implementation time: link developercertificate.org (DCO story not yet merged); link CONTRIBUTING.md §2c (review-policy story not yet merged); update these if those stories have landed before this PR is submitted.'
findings.finding: The plan's link-target instructions use conditional tense ('link CONTRIBUTING §2c now; will retarget once review-policy lands' / 'until then links developercertificate.org') without stating which state is the CURRENT default, so an executor must infer whether sibling stories have already landed.
findings.location: What section — beat 1 ('For vote semantics, link CONTRIBUTING.md §2c now') and beat 3 ('links CONTRIBUTING's DCO section once the DCO story lands — until then links developercertificate.org')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a small 'Implementation-time defaults' callout box listing the two conditional links with their current values and the condition under which each changes, so the executor has a single authoritative lookup.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a 'What if nothing happens?' paragraph (or gotchas row) covering the stale/no-response scenario: how long to wait, how to re-ping (comment on the Gerrit change or the GitHub issue), and how to abandon the change in Gerrit if the contributor withdraws.
findings.finding: The tutorial describes the 'your change is pushed and under review' state but provides no guidance for the abandon/stale-change scenario: what does an outsider do if days pass with no maintainer response, or if they want to withdraw their change from Gerrit? This is a real non-happy-path exit the tutorial is silent on.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the gotchas table: 'No response after N days — comment on the Gerrit change tagging a maintainer, or reply on the originating GitHub issue. To withdraw, click Abandon in the Gerrit UI — this closes the change without merging.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one dogfooding or before/after criterion: e.g., 'A maintainer or beta tester with no prior Gerrit access follows only this doc and reaches a submitted (or reviewer-approved) change' or 'Tested against a fresh GitHub account with no Nava Gerrit history.'
findings.finding: All Acceptance Criteria use static file-content checks (wc -w, grep) to validate the tutorial's value. There is no dogfooding criterion — no requirement that a real outsider (or a maintainer acting as one) walks through the tutorial end-to-end and successfully submits a change. Without this, the tutorial could pass every AC while still failing its primary purpose.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section — all six ACs

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
